metadata.name: Japanese Sentences
metadata.description: Ichidan verbs with counters, proper nouns, and culturally useful notes.

entries.ja: フーディーニは三回も冷たい水を浴びた。
entries.en: Houdini even bathed in cold water three times.
grammarNotes: 回 is a general counter for occurrences/actions.

chunks.ja: フーディーニは三回も

notes: Houdini is famous for extreme escape and endurance feats; 三回も adds surprise/emphasis (“as many as three times”).

entries.ja: 駅で友だちを二人迎えました。
entries.en: I welcomed two friends at the station.
grammarNotes: 人 is the counter for people (二人 = two people).
notes: 迎える is commonly used for meeting someone at a station/airport, not just greeting verbally.

entries.ja: 先生は三つの質問に答えた。
entries.en: The teacher answered three questions.
grammarNotes: つ is a general counter used for small quantities or when the specific counter is unknown.
notes: Japanese often omits the subject; here it is explicit for learning clarity.

entries.ja: 彼は概要を一ページで伝えた。
entries.en: He conveyed the summary in one page.
grammarNotes: ページ is a counter for pages/documents.

chunks.ja: 彼は概要を一ページで
chunks.en: he in one page the summary

notes: Japanese business communication often values concise summaries (概要) over long explanations.

entries.ja: 私は二分で住所を調べた。
entries.en: I looked up the address in two minutes.
grammarNotes: 分 is the counter for minutes when expressing duration.
notes: で after a time span marks the time required to complete an action.

entries.ja: このプリンとあのプリンを二個比べた。
entries.en: I compared these two puddings.
grammarNotes: 個 is a general counter for small objects.

chunks.ja: このプリンとあのプリンを二個

notes: プリン in Japan usually refers to custard pudding (flan-like), not gelatin dessert.

entries.ja: 彼は三枚の写真を見せた。
entries.en: He showed three photos.
grammarNotes: 枚 is the counter for flat objects (paper, photos, tickets).
notes: 見せる implies showing something to someone; the recipient is often omitted if obvious.

entries.ja: 会議の時間を一時間に決めた。
entries.en: We decided the meeting time to be one hour.
grammarNotes: 時間 is a counter for hours when expressing duration.

chunks.ja: 会議の時間を一時間に
chunks.en: the meeting time to one hour

notes: Japanese often omits “we”; the subject is inferred from context.

entries.ja: 彼は三日で研究を始めた。
entries.en: He started the research in three days.
grammarNotes: 日 is the counter for days when expressing time span.
notes: 三日で implies the preparation period before starting, not the duration of the research itself.

entries.ja: 彼女は二本の説明を終えた。
entries.en: She finished two explanations.
grammarNotes: 本 is a counter for long cylindrical items, but also used metaphorically for speeches/presentations.
notes: Using 本 for talks/presentations is metaphorical; learners often expect 回 instead.

entries.ja: 彼はフリーズを五秒続けた。
entries.en: He kept the freeze for five seconds.
grammarNotes: 秒 is the counter for seconds.
notes: フリーズ is a loanword used in gaming/dance contexts to mean “hold still.”

entries.ja: 先生は二点の努力を認めた。
entries.en: The teacher acknowledged two points of effort.
grammarNotes: 点 is a counter for points/items in lists or evaluations.

chunks.en: the teacher two points of effort

notes: 点 is common in grading or evaluations; it does not always mean physical ‘points.’

entries.ja: 彼女はプテラノドンの模型を一体褒めた。
entries.en: She praised one pteranodon model.
grammarNotes: 体 is a counter for large animals or statues/models of living beings.

chunks.ja: 彼女はプテラノドンの模型を一体

notes: 体 is also used for statues/figures of animals, not only living creatures.

entries.ja: 子どもは鬼をこわがっています。
entries.en: Children are afraid of demons.
grammarNotes: Vている here expresses a continuing state (being afraid), not an ongoing action.

entries.ja: 太郎は家の長男です。
entries.en: Tarō is the eldest son in the family.

chunks.en: is the eldest son of the family

entries.ja: 昔話には鬼がよく出てきます。
entries.en: Demons often appear in old folk tales.
grammarNotes: Vてくる often indicates something appearing/coming into existence from a narrative perspective.

entries.ja: 太郎は元気な子どもです。
entries.en: Tarō is an energetic child.

entries.ja: びっくりして目玉が大きくなった。
entries.en: My eyes widened in surprise.
grammarNotes: Vて links cause/reason to the following clause (because I was surprised...).
Vくなる expresses a change of state.

entries.ja: うちの親父は料理が好きです。
entries.en: My dad likes cooking.
notes: 親父 is colloquial and masculine; tone depends on context.

entries.ja: 彼は家の長男です。
entries.en: He is the eldest son in the family.

chunks.en: is the eldest son of the family

entries.ja: 長男なので、家をつぐ予定です。
entries.en: Because he is the eldest son, he plans to inherit the family.
grammarNotes: なので gives a softer explanatory reason than だから.

entries.ja: 長男は東京で働いています。
entries.en: The eldest son works in Tokyo.
grammarNotes: Vている indicates a continuing state (employment).

entries.ja: 河の近くで遊ばないでください。
entries.en: Please don't play near the river.
grammarNotes: Vないでください is a polite request not to do something.

entries.ja: 昔の童の話を読みました。
entries.en: I read an old story about children.

entries.ja: 河童は川に住むと言われています。
entries.en: Kappa are said said to live in rivers.
grammarNotes: と言われている expresses hearsay or commonly accepted belief.

entries.ja: 子どもに川へ近づかないように、河童の話をしました。
entries.en: We told kappa stories so children wouldn’t go near the river.
grammarNotes: Vないように expresses purpose/prevention ('so that not').

chunks.ja: 子どもに川へ近づかないように
chunks.en: so that children do not go near the river

entries.ja: 昔から河童の伝説が日本にあります。
entries.en: Kappa legends have existed in Japan since long ago.
grammarNotes: 昔から indicates continuation from the past until now.

chunks.ja: 河童の伝説が日本にあります

entries.ja: 森で狐を見ました。
entries.en: I saw a fox in the forest.

entries.ja: 妖狐は人に化けると言われています。
entries.en: Fox spirits are said to transform into humans.
grammarNotes: Vると言われている indicates commonly reported belief.

chunks.en: that fox spirits transform into people

entries.ja: 昔の話には狐の霊がよく出てきます。
entries.en: Fox spirits often appear in old stories.

entries.ja: 村の人は狐の霊を信じていました。
entries.en: The villagers believed in fox spirits.
grammarNotes: Vていた marks a past continuing state.

chunks.ja: 狐の霊を信じていました

entries.ja: 狐の霊が人に化けるという伝説があります。
entries.en: There is a legend that fox spirits transform into humans.
grammarNotes: という + noun labels or defines content ('the legend that...').

chunks.ja: 狐の霊が人に化けるという
chunks.en: that fox spirits transform into people

entries.ja: ハワイの海で三時間も泳いでいました。
entries.en: I was swimming in the ocean in Hawaii for three hours.
grammarNotes: Vていました = past progressive / ongoing action in the past.
Vていました

chunks.en: for as long as three hours

entries.ja: 東京駅まで急がなければなりません。
entries.en: I have to hurry to Tokyo Station.
grammarNotes: Vなければなりません = formal obligation “must do”.
Vなければなりません

chunks.ja: 急がなければなりません

entries.ja: 家に入ったら、すぐコートを脱いでください。
entries.en: When you enter the house, please take off your coat right away.
grammarNotes: Vたら = conditional “when/if”.
Vてください = polite request.
Vたら
Vてください

chunks.ja: コートを脱いでください

entries.ja: このアプリはウイルスを防げます。
entries.en: This app can prevent viruses.
grammarNotes: Potential form (Vえる/られる) expresses ability.
Potential

entries.ja: 彼はニューヨークでたくさんお金を稼いだ。
entries.en: He earned a lot of money in New York.
grammarNotes: Plain past (Vた) indicates completed action.
Vた

chunks.ja: たくさんお金を稼いだ

entries.ja: 子供たちが夜遅くまで騒いでいる。
entries.en: The children are being noisy until late at night.
grammarNotes: Vている can express ongoing action or current state.
Vている

entries.ja: Wi-Fiにスマホを繋げば、地図が見られます。
entries.en: If you connect your phone to Wi-Fi, you can see the map.
grammarNotes: Vば = conditional “if/when”.
Vば

chunks.en: if you connect your phone to Wi-Fi

entries.ja: 彼女は京都のカフェでコーヒーを注いでいた。
entries.en: She was pouring coffee at a café in Kyoto.
grammarNotes: Vていた = past ongoing action.
Vていた

chunks.ja: 彼女は京都のカフェで
chunks.en: she at a café in Kyoto

chunks.ja: コーヒーを注いでいた

entries.ja: 朝、包丁を研いでから料理を始めます。
entries.en: In the morning, I sharpen the knife and then start cooking.
grammarNotes: Vてから = do A and then B.
Vてから

chunks.en: in the morning, after sharpening the knife

entries.ja: このシャツは手で濯いだほうがいい。
entries.en: It’s better to rinse this shirt by hand.
grammarNotes: Vたほうがいい = giving advice or recommendation.
Vたほうがいい

chunks.ja: 手で濯いだほうがいい
chunks.en: it’s better to rinse by hand

entries.ja: 犬がバッグの中をずっと嗅いでいる。
entries.en: The dog has been sniffing inside the bag the whole time.
grammarNotes: Vている can describe an ongoing action in progress.
ずっと marks continuous duration over a period of time and here modifies the action, not the location phrase.
ずっと + Vている
notes: バッグ is a loanword and often suggests a personal bag or handbag in everyday usage.
バッグの中を indicates the area being sniffed through; ずっと applies to 嗅いでいる rather than to the noun phrase.

entries.ja: 彼は毎朝プールで泳ぐ。
entries.en: He swims in the pool every morning.
grammarNotes: Frequency adverb 毎朝 commonly pairs with habitual present.

entries.ja: 人混みの中を泳ぐように進んだ。
entries.en: I pushed forward through the crowd as if swimming.
grammarNotes: Vるように = 'as if / in such a way that'.
Vるように
notes: Figurative use of 泳ぐ for moving through a dense space.

entries.ja: 駅まで急いだ。
entries.en: I hurried to the station.

entries.ja: 宿題を急いで終わらせた。
entries.en: I hurried and finished my homework quickly.
grammarNotes: Vて = sequential action ('hurrying and then…').
Vて
notes: 終わらせる is transitive: 'finish (something)'.

entries.ja: 結論を急ぐべきではない。
entries.en: You shouldn't rush to a conclusion.
grammarNotes: Vるべきではない = 'should not'.
Vるべきではない

entries.ja: 靴を脱いでください。
entries.en: Please take off your shoes.
grammarNotes: Vてください = polite request.
Vてください
notes: Common in homes, some restaurants, and certain indoor spaces.

entries.ja: りんごの皮を脱ぐ。
entries.en: Peel off an apple’s skin.
notes: This use exists but 皮をむく is more common for 'peel'.

entries.ja: 事故を防ぐために注意する。
entries.en: Be careful in order to prevent accidents.
grammarNotes: Vるために = 'in order to'.
Vるために

entries.ja: 雨を防ぐコートを着る。
entries.en: I wear a coat that blocks the rain.
grammarNotes: Plain form + noun = noun modification.

entries.ja: 彼は会社でお金を稼いでいる。
entries.en: He earns money at his company.
grammarNotes: Vている can express ongoing state/habit.
Vている

entries.ja: 広告で時間を稼いだ。
entries.en: I bought time with an ad.
notes: Common in games/apps: watch an ad to get extra time/continues.

entries.ja: 子どもたちが外で騒いでいる。
entries.en: The kids are making noise outside.
grammarNotes: Vている = ongoing action.
Vている

entries.ja: そんなことで騒ぐな。
entries.en: Don’t make a fuss over something like that.
grammarNotes: Dictionary form + な = casual negative command.
Vるな

entries.ja: 心が騒いで眠れなかった。
entries.en: My mind was unsettled and I couldn’t sleep.
grammarNotes: Vられる(neg) = inability (眠れない).
Vて

entries.ja: ケーブルを機械に繋ぐ。
entries.en: Connect the cable to the machine.

entries.ja: 過去と未来を繋ぐ物語。
entries.en: A story that connects the past and the future.
grammarNotes: Plain form + noun = noun modification.

entries.ja: 話題を繋いで時間を稼ぐ。
entries.en: Keep the conversation going and buy time.
grammarNotes: Vて = sequence/means ('by doing X, do Y').
Vて

entries.ja: コップに水を注ぐ。
entries.en: Pour water into a cup.

entries.ja: 研究に力を注ぐ。
entries.en: Devote energy to research.

entries.ja: 窓から光が注いでいる。
entries.en: Light is streaming in through the window.
grammarNotes: Vている = ongoing state (streaming).
Vている

entries.ja: 包丁を研ぐ。
entries.en: Sharpen a kitchen knife.

entries.ja: 技術を研ぐ。
entries.en: Hone your skills.

entries.ja: シャツを水で濯ぐ。
entries.en: Rinse a shirt with water.

entries.ja: 食後に口を濯ぐ。
entries.en: Rinse your mouth after a meal.
grammarNotes: 食後 = 'after eating' (set phrase).

entries.ja: 花の香りを嗅ぐ。
entries.en: Smell the scent of a flower.

entries.ja: 危険を嗅ぎつける。
entries.en: Sense danger (sniff it out).
grammarNotes: Vます-stem + つける often means 'do thoroughly / attach' → idiomatic compounds.
notes: 嗅ぎつける is an idiom: detect/sense something quickly.

entries.ja: 家業を継ぐ。
entries.en: Inherit the family business.

entries.ja: 父の後を継ぐ。
entries.en: Succeed my father (take over after him).
grammarNotes: 後を継ぐ is a set phrase for succession.

entries.ja: 伝統を継ぐ。
entries.en: Carry on a tradition.

entries.ja: 布を継いで使う。
entries.en: Patch cloth together and use it.
grammarNotes: Vて = means ('by patching, use it').
Vて

entries.ja: 穴を塞ぐ。
entries.en: Block up a hole.

entries.ja: 耳を塞ぐ。
entries.en: Cover your ears.

entries.ja: 道を塞ぐ。
entries.en: Block the road.

entries.ja: 悲しくて胸が塞いだ。
entries.en: I was so sad that my chest felt heavy.
grammarNotes: くて = cause/reason (悲しくて…).
Aくて
notes: 胸が塞ぐ is a common expression for emotional heaviness.

entries.ja: 米袋を担ぐ。
entries.en: Shoulder a sack of rice.

entries.ja: みんなに担がれた。
entries.en: Everyone was playing a prank on me.
grammarNotes: Passive Vられる can mark being subjected to an action.
受け身
notes: 担ぐ = 'to prank/hoax' in this colloquial sense.

entries.ja: 神輿を担ぐ。
entries.en: Carry a portable shrine (mikoshi).
notes: Mikoshi are carried in festivals; teams lift and chant together.

entries.ja: じゃがいもの皮を剥ぐ。
entries.en: Peel a potato.

entries.ja: 仮面を剥ぐ。
entries.en: Strip off the mask (reveal the truth).
notes: Often used figuratively: expose someone's true nature.

entries.ja: 空を仰ぐ。
entries.en: Look up at the sky.

entries.ja: 師を仰ぐ。
entries.en: Revere a mentor.
notes: Somewhat formal/literary compared to 尊敬する.

entries.ja: 医者の助けを仰ぐ。
entries.en: Seek a doctor's help.
grammarNotes: Nの + N links possession/association (医者の助け).

entries.ja: ボートを漕ぐ。
entries.en: Row a boat.

entries.ja: 自転車を漕ぐ。
entries.en: Pedal a bicycle.

entries.ja: 困難の中を漕いで進む。
entries.en: Push forward through difficulties.
grammarNotes: Nの中を = 'through/within' a situation.
Vて
notes: Figurative extension from rowing/pedaling.

entries.ja: 神社に金を貢ぐ。
entries.en: Offer money to a shrine.
notes: Often implies offering/supporting rather than ordinary 'donation' wording.

entries.ja: 彼にお金を貢いでいる。
entries.en: She’s financially supporting him (one-sidedly).
grammarNotes: Vている = ongoing situation/habit.
Vている
notes: This sense often carries a negative nuance (being taken advantage of).

entries.ja: 糸を紡ぐ。
entries.en: Spin thread.

entries.ja: 言葉を紡いで語る。
entries.en: Weave words and speak.
grammarNotes: Vて = means ('by weaving words, speak').
Vて
notes: Literary feel; common in writing about storytelling.

entries.ja: 人生を紡ぐ。
entries.en: Build a life over time.
notes: Metaphorical; sounds poetic.

entries.ja: 木の表面を削ぐ。
entries.en: Shave off the surface of the wood.

entries.ja: やる気を削ぐ発言。
entries.en: A remark that kills motivation.
grammarNotes: Plain form + noun = noun modification (削ぐ発言).
notes: Common collocation: やる気を削ぐ = demotivate someone.

entries.ja: 庭で蜂を見たので、窓を閉めました。
entries.en: I saw a bee in the yard, so I closed the window.
notes: Uses ので for a neutral, explanatory “because,” fitting for giving a reason without sounding accusatory.
grammarNotes: ので is a softer, explanatory “because” than から and often sounds more neutral/polite.
AのでB

chunks.en: because I saw a bee in the yard,

entries.ja: 夏は蜂が多いから、甘い飲み物に気をつけて。
entries.en: There are lots of bees in summer, so be careful with sweet drinks.
notes: A casual warning; 気をつけて sounds natural in conversation.
grammarNotes: から gives a straightforward reason; it can sound more direct than ので.
気をつけて is a common casual imperative meaning “be careful.”
AからB

chunks.en: because there are many bees in summer,

entries.ja: その花には蜜蜂がたくさん集まっています。
entries.en: Many honeybees are gathering on that flower.
notes: には highlights the location/target where honeybees gather (on/around the flower).
grammarNotes: Vている can describe an ongoing state/result (here: “are gathering/clustered”).
Vている

entries.ja: 蜜蜂は花粉を運ぶので、農業にも大切です。
entries.en: Honeybees carry pollen, so they are important for agriculture too.
notes: も emphasizes “also/too,” adding agriculture as another relevant domain.
grammarNotes: ので is a neutral explanatory “because.”
にも adds “also/too” to a target (here: agriculture).
AのでB

chunks.ja: 蜜蜂は花粉を運ぶので、

entries.ja: のどが痛いとき、温かいお茶に蜂蜜を入れます。
entries.en: When my throat hurts, I add honey to warm tea.
notes: A common home remedy context: honey in warm tea for a sore throat.
grammarNotes: とき means “when” (time/occasion) and can set a general situation.
Aとき

entries.ja: ヨーグルトに蜂蜜をかけると食べやすいです。
entries.en: If you drizzle honey on yogurt, it’s easier to eat.
notes: A practical, everyday tip; 食べやすい expresses ease/comfort in eating.
grammarNotes: Vると can express a natural result: “when/if you do X, Y happens.”
やすい attaches to a verb stem to mean “easy to do” (食べやすい).
Vると

chunks.ja: ヨーグルトに蜂蜜をかけると
chunks.en: if you drizzle honey on yogurt,

entries.ja: 山で雀蜂を見かけたら、走らずに静かに離れてください。
entries.en: If you spot a hornet in the mountains, please don’t run—quietly move away.
notes: Safety instruction tone: 〜てください is polite and appropriate for guidance.
grammarNotes: Vたら is a common conditional for “if/when.”
〜ずに means “without doing” (走らずに = without running).
Vたら
Vずに

chunks.ja: 山で雀蜂を見かけたら、
chunks.en: if you spot a hornet in the mountains,

chunks.ja: 静かに離れてください。

entries.ja: 雀蜂は危ないので、巣を見つけたら市役所に連絡します。
entries.en: Hornets are dangerous, so if I find a nest, I contact city hall.
notes: 市役所 (city hall) is a realistic contact point in Japan for reporting hazards like nests.
grammarNotes: ので is an explanatory “because.”
Vたら is a common “if/when” conditional.
AのでB
Vたら

entries.ja: ベランダに蜂の巣ができていて、びっくりしました。
entries.en: There was a wasp nest on the balcony, and I was surprised.
notes: できている describes an established state: the nest has formed and exists there now.
grammarNotes: Vている can describe a resulting state (できている = has formed/is there).
〜ていて can connect clauses with a causal/scene-setting feel.
Vていて

chunks.ja: ベランダに蜂の巣ができていて、
chunks.en: there being a wasp nest on the balcony,

entries.ja: 蜂の巣が近いと危ないから、子どもには近づかないように言った。
entries.en: Because a nest nearby is dangerous, I told the kids not to go near it.
notes: 近づかないように言った is a common way to report giving a caution/instruction.
grammarNotes: Aと expresses a general condition: “if/when A, then (it is) B.”
〜ないように言う means “tell someone not to do” (caution/instruction).
から gives a direct reason.
AとB
Vないように言う

chunks.ja: 蜂の巣が近いと危ないから、
chunks.en: because it’s dangerous if a nest is nearby,

chunks.ja: 近づかないように言った。
chunks.en: I told them not to go near it.

entries.ja: 田中さんは東京で新しい仕事を掴んだ。
entries.en: Mr. Tanaka secured a new job in Tokyo.
grammarNotes: で marks the location where something happens.

entries.ja: 山田さんは進路について悩んでいる。
entries.en: Ms. Yamada is worrying about her future path.
grammarNotes: Vている shows an ongoing state or action.

chunks.ja: 山田さんは進路について
chunks.en: Ms. Yamada about her future path

entries.ja: 大阪のカフェでコーヒーを頼んだ。
entries.en: I ordered coffee at a café in Osaka.

entries.ja: ノートの字が雨で滲んだ。
entries.en: The writing in the notebook smudged because of the rain.

entries.ja: 鈴木さんは毎朝お茶を飲む。
entries.en: Mr. Suzuki drinks tea every morning.

entries.ja: 昨日から頭が少し痛む。
entries.en: My head has been hurting a little since yesterday.
grammarNotes: から marks the starting point in time.

entries.ja: 佐藤さんは箱を車に積んだ。
entries.en: Mr. Sato loaded the boxes into the car.

entries.ja: 古いテーブルが少し歪んでいる。
entries.en: The old table is slightly warped.
grammarNotes: Vている can describe a resulting state.

entries.ja: 彼は昔の失敗を悔む。
entries.en: He regrets his past mistakes.

entries.ja: このセーターは洗うと縮む。
entries.en: This sweater shrinks when washed.
grammarNotes: と can express a natural result or general truth.
Vると

entries.ja: 京都で起きた事故を多くの人が悔やんだ。
entries.en: Many people mourned the accident that happened in Kyoto.

chunks.en: the accident that happened in Kyoto

entries.ja: 中村さんは福岡に住んでいる。
entries.en: Mr. Nakamura lives in Fukuoka.

entries.ja: 二人でチームを組んだ。
entries.en: The two of us formed a team.
grammarNotes: 二人 is the counter for two people.

entries.ja: 午後になって雨が止んだ。
entries.en: The rain stopped in the afternoon.

entries.ja: 町の人々は有名な作家の死を悼んだ。
entries.en: The townspeople mourned the death of the famous writer.

chunks.ja: 有名な作家の死を悼んだ

entries.ja: 北海道は自然に富んでいる。
entries.en: Hokkaido is rich in nature.
grammarNotes: に富む means “to be rich in / abound in.”

entries.ja: 彼は質問に答えず、口を噤んだ。
entries.en: He didn’t answer the question and fell silent.
grammarNotes: ず is a formal negative connective meaning “without doing.”

entries.ja: 田村さんは甘い物を慎んでいる。
entries.en: Mr. Tamura is refraining from sweets.

entries.ja: 山口さんは小さな店を営んでいる。
entries.en: Mr. Yamaguchi runs a small shop.

chunks.ja: 山口さんは小さな店を

entries.ja: 彼は昔の上司を今でも恨んでいる。
entries.en: He still resents his former boss.

entries.ja: 先生は学生をじっと睨んだ。
entries.en: The teacher stared intensely at the student.

entries.ja: 健太くんは図書館で本を読む。
entries.en: Kenta reads books at the library.

entries.ja: 母はプレゼントをきれいな紙で包んだ。
entries.en: My mother wrapped the present in pretty paper.

entries.ja: 祖母は冬になるとマフラーを編む。
entries.en: My grandmother knits scarves when winter comes.
grammarNotes: と can express a habitual or natural occurrence.

entries.ja: 上野動物園でパンダの赤ちゃんが生まれた。
entries.en: A baby panda was born at Ueno Zoo.
notes: 生まれる is the intransitive counterpart of 生む.

chunks.ja: パンダの赤ちゃんが生まれた

entries.ja: 日曜日は家でゆっくり休む。
entries.en: I rest slowly at home on Sundays.

entries.ja: このバナナはもう傷んでいる。
entries.en: This banana has already gone bad.

entries.ja: 試験に落ちて、彼はしょげている。
entries.en: He failed the exam and is feeling down.
notes: しょげる is the standard modern form meaning to feel dejected.

entries.ja: 彼女は海外で働くことを望んでいる。
entries.en: She hopes to work abroad.

chunks.ja: 彼女は海外で働くことを

entries.ja: 駅で誰かの足を踏んでしまった。
entries.en: I accidentally stepped on someone's foot at the station.
grammarNotes: Vてしまう often expresses regret or accidental completion.

entries.ja: 庭に蜂が飛んでいるので、気をつけてください。
entries.en: A bee is flying in the garden, so please be careful.
grammarNotes: 〜ている indicates an ongoing state/action ("is flying").
ので gives an explanatory reason (so/because), often softer than から.
Vてください is a polite request.
Vている + ので
Vてください

entries.ja: 子どものころ、蜂に刺されたことがあります。
entries.en: I have been stung by a bee when I was a child.
grammarNotes: Vたことがある expresses past experience ("have ever done/experienced").
刺された is passive, emphasizing the experience of being stung.
Passive + Vたことがある

chunks.ja: 蜂に刺されたことがあります
chunks.en: I have been stung by a bee (before)

entries.ja: この店では地元の蜂蜜を売っています。
entries.en: This shop sells local honey.
grammarNotes: 〜では sets the topic/location context ("at this shop").
売っています is a common use of 〜ている for a habitual/ongoing business action ("they sell").
〜では (context/topic)
Vている (habitual/ongoing)

entries.ja: 彼女は問題の原因を一つ掴んだ。
entries.en: She grasped one cause of the problem.
notes: 掴む is used metaphorically for “grasping” an idea or key point, not only physically grabbing.
grammarNotes: 一つ is a general counter (つ) often used when the specific counter is not important.

chunks.ja: 彼女は問題の原因を一つ
chunks.en: she one cause of the problem

entries.ja: 私は三日も悩んで、やっと決めた。
entries.en: I worried for as many as three days and finally decided.
notes: 三日も adds emphasis/surprise: “as long as three days.”
grammarNotes: も after a number can add emphasis (e.g., “as many as/for as long as”).
やっと indicates something was achieved after difficulty or time.

chunks.en: I for as long as three days

entries.ja: 駅前の店でコーヒーを二杯頼んだ。
entries.en: I ordered two cups of coffee at the shop in front of the station.
notes: 頼む is natural for ordering food/drinks in restaurants or cafes.
grammarNotes: 杯 is a counter for cups/glasses (二杯 = two cups).
で marks the location where an action takes place.

chunks.en: at the shop in front of the station

entries.ja: インクが二か所も滲んで、字が読みにくい。
entries.en: The ink smudged in as many as two places, so the writing is hard to read.
notes: 二か所も emphasizes that there were “as many as two spots.”
grammarNotes: か所 is a counter for places/points (二か所 = two places).
Vて is commonly used to show cause/result with a following clause.

chunks.en: ink in as many as two places
the letters are hard to read

entries.ja: 薬を一回飲んだら、少し楽になった。
entries.en: After I took the medicine once, I felt a little better.
notes: 飲む is used for taking medicine as well as drinking.
grammarNotes: 回 is a general counter for occurrences/actions (一回 = once).
Vたら can mark a condition or sequence (“when/after doing”).
Vたら

entries.ja: 階段を五段上っただけで、足が痛む。
entries.en: Just climbing five steps of stairs makes my legs hurt.
notes: 五段 is natural when counting steps/tiers; in daily speech people also say 五段目まで, etc.
grammarNotes: だけで means “just by/only,” often implying it was enough to cause the result.

entries.ja: 彼は箱を三つ積んで、車に運んだ。
entries.en: He stacked three boxes and carried them to the car.
notes: 三つ is a general counter used for items when the specific counter is not emphasized.
grammarNotes: つ is a general counter (三つ = three items).
Vて links actions in sequence.

entries.ja: 写真が少し歪んで見えるのは、このレンズのせいだ。
entries.en: The reason the photo looks slightly distorted is because of this lens.
notes: のは〜せいだ is a common way to explain a cause in a firm/explicit tone.
grammarNotes: Vのは～だ nominalizes the clause and sets up an explanation.
せいだ expresses a cause and can sound blaming depending on context.
Vのは～だ

chunks.ja: 写真が少し歪んで見えるのは
chunks.en: that the photo looks slightly distorted

entries.ja: 彼は一言も言わずに、口を噤んだ。
entries.en: Without saying a single word, he fell silent.
notes: 噤む is somewhat literary; 口をつぐむ is a more everyday phrasing.
grammarNotes: 一言も～ない expresses “not even a single word.”
ずに means “without doing.”

chunks.en: he without saying even one word

entries.ja: この会社は社員教育に力を入れて、経験に富む人材を育てている。
entries.en: This company focuses on employee training and develops talent rich in experience.
notes: 〜に富む is common in formal/business writing.
grammarNotes: に富む is a set expression meaning “be rich in/abound in.”
力を入れる means “to put effort into.”

chunks.ja: この会社は社員教育に力を入れて
chunks.en: this company puts effort into employee training

entries.ja: 彼は夜遅い外出を慎んで、毎朝早く起きるようにしている。
entries.en: He refrains from going out late at night and tries to wake up early every morning.
notes: 慎む can sound a bit formal; it fits well with self-discipline contexts.
grammarNotes: ようにしている expresses an ongoing effort or habit.
ようにしている

chunks.ja: 彼は夜遅い外出を慎んで
毎朝早く起きるようにしている
chunks.en: tries to wake up early every morning

entries.ja: 両親は小さな店を営んで、私を大学まで通わせてくれた。
entries.en: My parents ran a small shop and supported me through college.
notes: 〜まで通わせてくれた implies support (money/time/effort) rather than only permission.
grammarNotes: Vてくれる indicates the action was done for the speaker’s benefit.
まで marks an endpoint/extent (up to college).

chunks.ja: 両親は小さな店を営んで
chunks.en: my parents ran a small shop

chunks.ja: 私を大学まで通わせてくれた

entries.ja: 彼は私を睨んでから、何も言わずに席を立った。
entries.en: He glared at me and then stood up without saying anything.
notes: から here indicates sequence (“after doing”), not only reason.
grammarNotes: Vてから indicates doing A and then B.
何も～ない expresses “not anything.”
Vてから

entries.ja: 私は資料を二ページでまとめて、会議で説明した。
entries.en: I summarized the materials in two pages and explained them at the meeting.
notes: まとめる is common for condensing information into a short form.
grammarNotes: ページ is a counter for pages/documents.
で after a measure can indicate the range/limit used (e.g., “within two pages”).

chunks.ja: 私は資料を二ページで
chunks.en: I within two pages the materials

entries.ja: 彼女は景色を望む部屋を一泊で予約した。
entries.en: She booked a room with a view of the scenery for one night.
notes: 望む can mean “to overlook/command a view,” common in listings (景色を望む部屋).
grammarNotes: 泊 is a counter for nights of stay (一泊 = one night).

chunks.ja: 彼女は景色を望む部屋を
chunks.en: she a room overlooking the scenery

entries.ja: 雨がやんだら、近所を一周走ろう。
entries.en: When the rain stops, let's run one lap around the neighborhood.
notes: 一周 is used for one full loop around an area; common for jogging routes.
grammarNotes: Vたら can express a condition (“when/if”).
う(意向形) suggests intention; here 走ろう is a proposal (“let’s”).
Vたら

entries.ja: この肉は二日で傷んだので、捨てるしかなかった。
entries.en: The meat went bad in two days, so I had no choice but to throw it away.
notes: ので is often used for a more explanatory/soft reason than から.
grammarNotes: で after a time span marks the time required for a change to occur.
しか～ない means “have no choice but to.”
ので vs から: ので is typically softer/more explanatory.

chunks.en: had no choice but to throw it away

entries.ja: このアプリは無料です。
entries.en: This app is free.
notes: 無料 is commonly used for apps, services, events, and trial offerings.
grammarNotes: 無料 is a noun/na-adjective meaning “free of charge.”
です gives a polite plain statement.

entries.ja: 今日は忙しいから、ゲームは駄目です。
entries.en: I’m busy today, so games are not allowed.
notes: 駄目 here means something is not okay or not permitted in the situation, not just “useless.”
grammarNotes: から marks a direct reason or cause.
駄目 is often used to say something is not allowed, not possible, or no good.
N/今日は + Aい + から

entries.ja: それは無駄な努力だ。
entries.en: That is a waste of effort.
notes: 無駄な努力 is a common collocation for effort that will not lead to a useful result.
grammarNotes: 無駄 is a na-adjective here: 無駄な + noun.
だ is the plain copula.

entries.ja: 無駄な時間を過ごしたくない。
entries.en: I don’t want to spend time pointlessly.
notes: 無駄な時間 refers to time that feels unproductive or not worthwhile.
grammarNotes: 無駄な + noun modifies 時間.
Vたくない expresses not wanting to do something.

entries.ja: お金を無駄に使わないでください。
entries.en: Please don’t waste money.
notes: 無駄に can describe doing something in a wasteful or pointless way.
grammarNotes: 無駄に acts adverbially here: “wastefully” / “for nothing.”
Vないでください is a polite request not to do something.
Vないでください

chunks.ja: 無駄に使わないでください

entries.ja: この会議は無駄だった。
entries.en: This meeting was pointless.
notes: This can imply the meeting produced no useful result or did not need to happen.
grammarNotes: 無駄だった is the past plain form of the na-adjective/noun predicate.
だった gives an informal past statement.

entries.ja: 大切な時間を無駄にした。
entries.en: I wasted precious time.
notes: 大切な時間 adds emotional weight, suggesting regret about losing something valuable.
grammarNotes: 無駄にする means “to waste” something.
大切な + noun means “important/precious + noun.”

entries.ja: 京都でお寺を三つ回りました。
entries.en: I visited three temples in Kyoto.
notes: 回る with places often means going around from one place to another, such as sightseeing stops.
grammarNotes: つ is a general native Japanese counter often used for small numbers of objects.
で after a place marks where the activity takes place.

entries.ja: 祖母はみかんを五個むきました。
entries.en: My grandmother peeled five mandarins.
notes: みかん commonly refers to Japanese mandarins and is a familiar winter fruit in Japan.
grammarNotes: 個 is a common counter for small concrete objects.

entries.ja: 先生はプリントを六枚配りました。
entries.en: The teacher handed out six printouts.
notes: プリント in school contexts often means a worksheet or handout rather than a 'print' in the art sense.
grammarNotes: 枚 is the counter for flat objects such as paper and sheets.

chunks.ja: 先生はプリントを六枚

entries.ja: 姉は映画館でチケットを二枚買いました。
entries.en: My older sister bought two tickets at the movie theater.
notes: 二枚 suggests two physical tickets, often for two people going together.
grammarNotes: 枚 is used for thin flat items such as tickets and paper.
で after a place marks where the action happens.

chunks.ja: 姉は映画館でチケットを二枚
chunks.en: my older sister two tickets at the movie theater

entries.ja: 田中さんは会議で資料を一部配った。
entries.en: Mr. Tanaka handed out one set of materials at the meeting.
notes: 資料 is a common business and school word for printed materials, reference documents, or handouts.
grammarNotes: 部 is a counter used for copies or sets of documents such as printed materials.

chunks.ja: 田中さんは会議で資料を一部
chunks.en: Mr. Tanaka one set of materials at the meeting

entries.ja: 鳥が私たちの真上を飛んでいる。
entries.en: A bird is flying directly above us.
grammarNotes: 真上 emphasizes something being directly overhead or vertically above.
Vている can describe an ongoing action or visible current state.
notes: を with a location can mark the path an action moves through or across.

entries.ja: ライトがテーブルの真上にある。
entries.en: The light is directly above the table.
grammarNotes: 真上 emphasizes exact vertical position above something.
にある marks the location of an inanimate object.

chunks.ja: ライトがテーブルの真上に
chunks.en: the light directly above the table

notes: ライト could refer to a ceiling light or lamp depending on context.

entries.ja: 飛行機が家の真上を通った。
entries.en: A plane passed directly over the house.
grammarNotes: 真上 marks exact vertical position over something.
を with movement verbs can mark the route or area something passes through.

chunks.en: a plane directly over the house

notes: 通る is commonly used for things passing through a space such as roads, trains, or airplanes overhead.

entries.ja: 月がビルの真上に見える。
entries.en: The moon appears directly above the building.
grammarNotes: 真上 expresses exact vertical position above something.
見える indicates something is visible or appears to the observer.

chunks.en: the moon directly above the building

notes: 見える focuses on something being visible to the viewer rather than the act of intentionally looking.

entries.ja: 手がべたべたする。
entries.en: My hands feel sticky.
notes: This is a very common everyday way to describe a sticky feeling on the hands.
grammarNotes: Aが〜する can describe a bodily or surface condition being felt directly.
べたべたする expresses an ongoing sticky/tacky sensation.
オノマトペ + する

entries.ja: 暑くて、体がべたべたしている。
entries.en: It’s hot, and my body feels sticky.
notes: This often describes the unpleasant sticky feeling caused by heat and sweat.
grammarNotes: 〜くて links an i-adjective to a following result or related state.
Vている here describes a current ongoing condition.
い-adjective + くて
オノマトペ + している

chunks.en: because it is hot / it is hot and

entries.ja: テーブルがジュースでべたべただ。
entries.en: The table is sticky with juice.
notes: 〜で marks the cause or substance creating the sticky state.
grammarNotes: Nで can indicate the cause/material responsible for a resulting state.
べたべただ uses the mimetic word as a predicative state.

entries.ja: 子どもが母親にべたべたしている。
entries.en: The child is clinging to their mother.
notes: Here べたべた is not about physical stickiness but clingy physical closeness.
grammarNotes: Nに with べたべたする marks the person someone is clinging to.
Vている shows the behavior is currently happening.
オノマトペ + している

entries.ja: 彼は彼女にべたべたしすぎる。
entries.en: He is too clingy with his girlfriend.
notes: This can sound critical, suggesting excessive physical closeness or affection.
grammarNotes: Vます-stem + すぎる means 'do too much' or 'be excessively ~'.
Nに with べたべたする marks the target of clingy behavior.
Vます-stem + すぎる

entries.ja: 手が油でべたべただ。
entries.en: My hands are greasy and sticky with oil.
notes: This often implies an unpleasant oily residue on the hands.
grammarNotes: Nで can mark the substance causing the condition.
べたべただ describes the resulting sticky state directly.

entries.ja: 床がジュースでべたべたになった。
entries.en: The floor became sticky from juice.
notes: This describes a change into a sticky state, often after something was spilled.
grammarNotes: A/N + になる means 'become ~' and marks a change of state.
Nで marks the cause or substance behind that change.
A/N + になる

entries.ja: 彼女にべたべたするな。
entries.en: Don’t cling all over her.
notes: This is a blunt command and can sound rough depending on context.
grammarNotes: Vる + な is a strong casual negative command.
Nに with べたべたする marks the person being clung to.
Vる + な

entries.ja: 日本にいる間、ずっと雨でした。
entries.en: It rained the whole time I was in Japan.
notes: ずっと marks uninterrupted duration across the entire stay in Japan.
いる間 naturally frames a bounded period: the speaker's time in Japan.
grammarNotes: A間、B expresses that B continues during the period A.
ずっと often means 'the whole time' when paired with a time span.
A間、B

chunks.en: it was raining the whole time

entries.ja: ずっとここに住みたい。
entries.en: I want to live here forever.
notes: ずっと here suggests an indefinitely long continuation rather than just 'for a while'.
The subject is omitted, which is natural in Japanese when understood from context.
grammarNotes: Vます-stem + たい expresses the speaker's desire to do something.
ずっと before a verb can mean 'continuously' or 'for a long time'.
Vたい

entries.ja: この本は前のよりずっと面白い。
entries.en: This book is much more interesting than the previous one.
notes: 前の stands for 'the previous one/book' without repeating 本.
ずっと in comparisons means 'far' or 'much' rather than duration.
grammarNotes: AはBよりC expresses comparison: A is more C than B.
ずっと can strongly intensify a comparison, similar to 'much' or 'far'.
AよりB

entries.ja: 彼は会議の間、ずっと黙っていた。
entries.en: He stayed silent the whole meeting.
notes: 黙っていた describes a continuing state of remaining silent, not just one moment of silence.
会議の間 sets a clear time frame over which the silence continued.
grammarNotes: A間、B marks that B continues throughout the period A.
Vていた often describes a continuing state in the past.
A間、B

entries.ja: 子供のころから、ずっとこの町に住んでいる。
entries.en: I have lived in this town since I was a child.
notes: 子供のころから gives the starting point of a long-lasting situation.
住んでいる here indicates an ongoing state of residence.
grammarNotes: Aから marks the starting point in time or space.
Vている can describe an ongoing state, such as living somewhere.

chunks.en: all this time in this town

entries.ja: 今日は昨日よりずっと寒い。
entries.en: Today is much colder than yesterday.
notes: A very common everyday comparison pattern in spoken and written Japanese.
ずっと adds clear degree emphasis, not time meaning.
grammarNotes: AはBよりC is the standard comparative pattern.
ずっと with comparatives means 'much/far more' rather than 'continuously'.
AよりB

entries.ja: 映画を見ている間、彼女はずっと泣いていた。
entries.en: She cried the whole time during the movie.
notes: 見ている間 emphasizes the span while the movie was being watched.
泣いていた suggests continuous crying over that period, not a single brief moment.
grammarNotes: Vている間に/間, another action or state continues during that time.
Vていた often expresses a continuing action or state in the past.
Vている間

entries.ja: ずっと先のことは、まだ分からない。
entries.en: I still do not know what will happen far in the future.
notes: ずっと先のこと refers to matters in the distant future, not something happening soon.
grammarNotes: 〜のこと can nominalize a broad matter/topic, as in 'the matter of ~' or 'what concerns ~'.
まだ〜ない means 'not yet'.

chunks.en: as for things far in the future

entries.ja: 彼はずっと先の計画まで考えている。
entries.en: He is thinking even about plans far into the future.
notes: まで adds the nuance that he is thinking as far as long-term plans, not just immediate ones.
grammarNotes: Vている can describe an ongoing mental activity or state.
〜まで adds extent, often meaning 'even as far as ~'.
Vている

entries.ja: ずっと先になってから、その意味が分かった。
entries.en: I understood its meaning much later.
notes: This implies a significant delay before understanding came.
grammarNotes: Nになってから means 'after it became ~' or 'once it reached ~'.
〜てから marks a sequence: first one stage happens, then the next.
Nになってから

chunks.ja: ずっと先になってから

entries.ja: この問題はずっと先まで影響する。
entries.en: This problem will affect things far into the future.
notes: The sentence suggests long-term consequences rather than a short-lived effect.
grammarNotes: 〜まで can indicate the limit or extent of time, as in 'up to / into ~'.

entries.ja: 彼は父の家を継いだ。
entries.en: He inherited his father's family household.
notes: 家を継ぐ refers to succeeding the family line or household, not simply receiving a building as property.
grammarNotes: 家を継ぐ often means to inherit the household, family line, or family role.
Vた is the plain past form used for completed actions.

entries.ja: 長男が家を継ぐことが多かった。
entries.en: The eldest son often inherited the family line.
notes: This reflects a traditional pattern in older Japanese family systems, where the eldest son was often expected to become the heir.
grammarNotes: Vることが多い means something often happens or is often the case.
長男 refers specifically to the eldest son.
Vることが多い

chunks.ja: 長男が家を継ぐことが
chunks.en: the eldest son inheriting the family was

entries.ja: 彼女は店と家を継いだ。
entries.en: She inherited both the shop and the family household.
notes: In context, 家 suggests family succession, while 店 can imply taking over a family-run business as well.
grammarNotes: AとB marks an exhaustive pairing: A and B.
When 継ぐ is used with a business or household, it often implies taking over responsibility as well as ownership.

chunks.en: the shop and the family household

entries.ja: 彼は父の跡を継いだ。
entries.en: He succeeded his father.
notes: 跡を継ぐ is a common expression for succeeding someone in their role, position, business, or family line.
grammarNotes: 跡を継ぐ focuses on succeeding a predecessor, while 家を継ぐ focuses more specifically on the household or family line.
父の marks possession or association: father's.

entries.ja: 父の仕事を継ぐつもりだ。
entries.en: I intend to take over my father's work.
notes: 仕事を継ぐ can refer to carrying on a parent's occupation, trade, or family business.
grammarNotes: Vるつもりだ means to intend to do something.
仕事 can mean job, work, trade, or professional role depending on context.
Vるつもりだ

entries.ja: 日本語で少しギリシャ神話について話してください。
entries.en: Please talk a little about Greek mythology in Japanese.
grammarNotes: Vてください is a polite request pattern.
Nについて expresses the topic 'about N'.

chunks.ja: ギリシャ神話について

notes: ギリシャ神話 is the standard term for Greek mythology; 伝説 would imply isolated legends rather than the mythological system.

entries.ja: ギリシャ神話は古代ギリシャの神々や英雄についての物語です。
entries.en: Greek mythology is a collection of stories about the gods and heroes of ancient Greece.
grammarNotes: NやN lists representative examples (A and B among others).
NについてのN forms 'a noun about N'.

chunks.ja: 古代ギリシャの神々や英雄についての
chunks.en: about the gods and heroes of ancient Greece

notes: 神々 is the pluralized form of 神 (god), commonly used when referring to mythological pantheons.

entries.ja: 一番有名な神はゼウスです。
entries.en: The most famous god is Zeus.
grammarNotes: 一番 is used to indicate the superlative ('the most').
notes: ゼウス (Zeus) is the chief god in Greek mythology and ruler of the Olympian gods.

entries.ja: オリンポス山には多くの神が住んでいると言われています。
entries.en: It is said that many gods live on Mount Olympus.
grammarNotes: Vている expresses an ongoing state.
と言われている is used for commonly accepted statements or traditions.

chunks.ja: 多くの神が住んでいる

notes: オリンポス山 (Mount Olympus) was believed to be the home of the main Greek gods.

entries.ja: ヘラクレスは十二の功業で有名な英雄です。
entries.en: Heracles is a hero famous for the Twelve Labors.
grammarNotes: Nで有名 expresses 'famous for N'.
notes: 十二の功業 refers to the Twelve Labors of Heracles, a famous cycle of heroic tasks in Greek mythology.

entries.ja: 「[LG] webOS TV OLED55B7A」でYouTubeにログインしましょう。
entries.en: Let’s sign in to YouTube on the [LG] webOS TV OLED55B7A.
notes: This is instructional UI language inviting the user to sign in on a specific TV model.
ましょう is used here as a soft prompt or call to action, common in device menus and setup screens.
grammarNotes: Vましょう can mean either 'let’s do' or a polite prompt like 'please do' in instructions.
で marks the device or place used for an action.
Vましょう

chunks.ja: YouTubeにログインしましょう

entries.ja: テレビでログインすれば、お気に入りの動画や登録チャンネルをご自宅の大画面で視聴できます。
entries.en: If you sign in on your TV, you can watch your favorite videos and subscribed channels on your home big screen.
notes: This sentence explains the benefit of signing in: personalized content becomes available on the TV.
登録チャンネル refers to channels the user has subscribed to on YouTube.
ご自宅 is an honorific/polite way to say 'your home,' often used in product copy.
grammarNotes: Vば expresses a conditional meaning like 'if/when.'
や links representative items in a non-exhaustive list.
Vことができる and Vできる express ability; here 視聴できます means 'can watch/view.'
Vば

chunks.ja: テレビでログインすれば、
chunks.en: if you sign in on the TV

chunks.ja: お気に入りの動画や登録チャンネルを

entries.ja: 彼は会議で新しいアイデアを持ち出した。
entries.en: He brought up a new idea in the meeting.
notes: 持ち出した here means bringing up a topic for discussion rather than physically carrying something out.
grammarNotes: で marks the setting or context where an action takes place.
持ち出す can mean either physically taking something out or raising a topic, depending on context.

entries.ja: 図書館の本は外に持ち出してはいけません。
entries.en: You must not take library books outside.
notes: This is a common rule-like sentence seen in libraries, schools, and shared spaces.
grammarNotes: Vてはいけません expresses prohibition or strong disallowance.
外に gives the destination or direction of taking something out.
Vてはいけません

chunks.ja: 持ち出してはいけません

entries.ja: 古い写真を箱から持ち出した。
entries.en: I took the old photos out of the box.
notes: The subject is omitted, which is natural in Japanese when it is clear from context.
grammarNotes: から marks the starting point or source from which something is taken.

entries.ja: 彼女は突然その問題を持ち出した。
entries.en: She suddenly brought up that issue.
notes: 持ち出した here suggests that the topic was raised, possibly unexpectedly or at an awkward moment.
grammarNotes: 突然 often adds a sense of unexpectedness to the action.
その before a noun often refers to something already known in the conversation or context.

entries.ja: 彼は会議の前に腕時計を外した。
entries.en: He took off his wristwatch before the meeting.
notes: 外す commonly refers to removing worn items like watches, glasses, or shoes.
grammarNotes: V前に indicates an action occurring before another action.

entries.ja: ボタンが一つ外れてしまった。
entries.en: One button came off.
notes: しまった adds a nuance that the speaker regrets or did not intend the result.
grammarNotes: Vてしまう often conveys completion with regret or accident.
Vてしまう

entries.ja: 今日は外で昼ご飯を食べよう。
entries.en: Let’s eat lunch outside today.
notes: 外 often refers to being outdoors rather than inside a building.
grammarNotes: Volitional form ～よう expresses suggestion or invitation.
Vよう

entries.ja: 彼女は外国で三年間働いた。
entries.en: She worked abroad for three years.
notes: 外国 emphasizes being outside one's own country.
grammarNotes: 年間 expresses a duration in years.

entries.ja: 駅の外側で友達を待っている。
entries.en: I’m waiting for my friend on the outside of the station.
notes: 外側 emphasizes the outer side or exterior area of something.
grammarNotes: Vている expresses a continuing state or ongoing action.
Vている

chunks.en: on the outside of the station

entries.ja: 外国人の観光客がこの町に増えている。
entries.en: Foreign tourists are increasing in this town.
notes: 外国人 literally means 'person from another country' and is widely used in everyday speech.
grammarNotes: Vている can describe a gradual change or trend.
Vている

entries.ja: 午後は少し外出する予定です。
entries.en: I plan to go out a little this afternoon.
notes: 外出 is often used in slightly formal contexts like schedules or announcements.
grammarNotes: 予定です expresses a planned action.
Nする予定

entries.ja: 日曜日以外は毎日働いている。
entries.en: I work every day except Sunday.
notes: 以外 marks exclusion, meaning 'other than' or 'except'.
grammarNotes: N以外 expresses exclusion ('except for N').
毎日 indicates habitual repetition.
N以外
Vている
